steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

Shell commands must end with \n. Special keys must be sent alone without \n, e.g. {"keystrokes":"C-c","duration":0.5}.

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
I have downloaded the gpt-2 weights stored as a TF .ckpt. Write me a dependency-free C file that samples from the model with arg-max sampling. Call your program /app/gpt2.c, I will compile with gcc -O3 -lm. It should read the .ckpt and the .bpe file. Your c program must be <5000 bytes. I will run it /app/a.out gpt2-124M.ckpt vocab.bpe "[input string here]" and you should continue the output under whatever GPT-2 would print for the next 20 tokens.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@0843f5a32dd5:/app#

Analysis: We are at `/app` with no commands run yet. The task requires creating `/app/gpt2.c` (<5000 bytes, dependency-free) that reads a TensorFlow GPT-2 `.ckpt` and `vocab.bpe`, encodes an input string, runs 20 arg-max decoding steps, and prints the continuation. First I need to inspect available files, tools, and the exact checkpoint/BPE formats before writing the C parser and model forward pass.
Plan: Run directory listings and tool checks. Inspect sizes/types of files in `/app`, verify `gcc`/`python3` availability, and look for the checkpoint and BPE files. This will determine the formats the C program must parse.

arguments.keystrokes: which gcc python3 file strings xxd || true

steps.message:
Analysis: We are in `/app` with only `gpt2-124M.ckpt` (497,759,232 bytes) and `vocab.bpe` (50,001 lines). The checkpoint size is exactly 124,439,808 float32 values, close to GPT-2 124M, but we still need to confirm whether there is metadata or a raw tensor layout. We also need to inspect BPE head/tail/special token line numbers. Previous batches failed due to JSON truncation around special characters, so I will use only safe single-line commands.
Plan: Run safe inspection commands: compute float count, dump first floats as decimal, inspect checkpoint tail, search for tensor-name strings and metadata markers, list files, inspect BPE head/tail, and sample specific BPE lines. These outputs will determine the C loader/tokenizer design.

Analysis: The last command batch partially ran. We confirmed the checkpoint is exactly 124,439,808 float32 values with no remainder and no visible metadata in the tail. The `strings` output did not show tensor names, suggesting a raw float32 dump, but the remaining BPE inspection commands were not executed or were mixed into the prompt. We still need to inspect BPE lines and then write/test a compact C implementation.
Plan: Run simple BPE inspection commands and a broad ASCII metadata search. Then use a Python helper to derive the exact raw tensor layout by mapping GPT-2 124M parameter counts to the float count and known shapes. After that, write a compact C program implementing BPE encoding, raw checkpoint loading, GPT-2 forward pass, and argmax decoding for 20 tokens.

steps.message:
Analysis: We need to determine the exact tensor layout of the raw checkpoint before finalizing the C model. The file is exactly 124,439,808 float32 values, matching GPT-2 124M if biases are included. I will use compact C probes to print offsets/ranges of likely tensors and test token-embedding dot products against a few known token strings. This will verify the canonical raw layout and guide the implementation.
Plan: Create and compile a small probe program `/tmp/probe.c` that mmaps the checkpoint, defines the standard GPT-2 parameter offsets, and prints counts/offsets plus row norms and dot products for first token rows. Run it to verify that the first tensor is the token embedding matrix. Then use that verified layout to implement `/app/gpt2.c`.

Analysis: The first probe failed due to C syntax/type errors. The checkpoint is exactly 124,439,808 float32 values with no metadata in the tail. The likely layout is raw GPT-2 124M weights: wte, wpe, then 12 transformer blocks, then final LayerNorm. I need to run a corrected probe to verify the exact offsets and end point before writing the final C program. BPE inspection showed `vocab.bpe` has 50,001 lines, standard GPT-2 merges, and no visible endoftext token line; special token handling must be inferred from token IDs.
Plan: Write a corrected `/tmp/probe.c` that mmaps the checkpoint to `float *`, walks the canonical GPT-2 layout, prints all key tensor offsets and the final end offset, and computes row norms for the first token embeddings. Compile and run it to verify layout. This is the smallest concrete next action needed before implementing `/app/gpt2.c`.

Analysis: The checkpoint layout is verified: raw float32 GPT-2 124M weights exactly matching 124,439,808 floats. The remaining critical work is to create a correct, compact `/app/gpt2.c` that implements byte-level BPE encoding, full GPT-2 forward inference, argmax decoding for 20 tokens, and token decoding. No source exists yet in the current terminal state. I will write a fresh implementation carefully, then compile, check size, and test behavior.
Plan: Create `/app/gpt2.c` with: hardcoded GPT-2 byte-to-unicode mapping, `vocab.bpe` parsing for merge ranks, BPE encoding using merge rank lookup, mmap checkpoint loading with verified offsets, full 12-layer causal self-attention and MLP forward pass, final LayerNorm, logits via token embedding transpose, argmax, and token string decoding. Then compile with `gcc -O3 -lm`, check source size with `wc -c`, and run a sample prompt to verify it produces English-like output.

Analysis: The current `/app/gpt2.c` is an incomplete skeleton and does not compile. We have verified the checkpoint layout: raw float32 GPT-2 124M weights starting with wte at offset 0, wpe at 38597376, blocks at 39383808, final ln at 124438272, totaling 124439808 floats. The BPE file is standard GPT-2 merges with 50,001 lines. A complete rewrite is needed with canonical byte-to-unicode BPE, full 12-layer causal attention, GELU MLP, argmax decoding, and token decoding. It must compile with `gcc -O3 -lm` and be under 5000 bytes.
Plan: Replace `/app/gpt2.c` with a complete compact implementation using global static arrays to avoid stack overflow. It will initialize GPT-2 byte-to-unicode, load merges, encode input with lowest-rank BPE merging, run full recompute inference for 20 steps, argmax logits, and decode token strings back to UTF-8 bytes. Then compile, check size, and test with a sample prompt.

Analysis: The current `/app/gpt2.c` does not compile because `M` is undefined. More importantly, the existing implementation has deeper correctness bugs: it reads `vocab.bpe` as 50,000 token lines followed by merges, but the file has only a header plus 50,000 merges. Its token table is incomplete/incorrect, and attention uses a per-head 64-dim cache instead of full 768-dim K/V per token. I need a corrected compact rewrite with canonical GPT-2 byte-to-unicode mapping, proper merge parsing/ID assignment, full D-dimensional K/V cache, concatenated attention output with single `c_proj`, GELU MLP, final LayerNorm, argmax decoding, and byte-accurate token decoding.
Plan: Replace `/app/gpt2.c` with a complete corrected implementation. Compile with `gcc -O3 -lm`, check source size, and run a sample prompt. This rewrite verifies behavior through the requested observable interface.

steps.message:
Analysis: The current `/app/gpt2.c` does not compile due to a typo and malformed loop. The checkpoint layout is verified: raw float32 GPT-2 124M weights with wte at 0, wpe at 38597376, blocks at 39383808, final ln at 124438272, total 124439808 floats. The BPE file is standard GPT-2 merges. A complete corrected implementation is needed with canonical vocab construction, full forward pass, argmax decoding, and token decoding. Previous panel suggestions were incomplete/truncated.
Plan: Replace `/app/gpt2.c` with a complete compact implementation. It will build base tokens 0-256, construct tokens 257-50255 from merge rules, encode input with lowest-rank merges, run 12-layer causal attention with full D-dim K/V cache, GELU MLP, final LayerNorm, argmax logits over token embeddings, and decode token strings back to bytes. Then compile, check size, and run a test prompt.

Analysis: The current `/app/gpt2.c` is 4835 bytes but does not compile due to pointer arithmetic in `build_vocab` (`lb=n-q-1`). A minimal patch should fix `lb` to `n-(q-p)-1`. I will also patch the `detok` boundary from `i+1<=n` to `i+1<n` to avoid reading past the final byte. Then I will recompile, check size, and run the required interface with a sample prompt.
Plan: Apply two small `sed` replacements to `/app/gpt2.c`: fix `lb` calculation and fix `detok` boundary. Recompile with `gcc -O3 -lm`, check source size, and run `/app/a.out /app/gpt2-124M.ckpt /app/vocab.bpe "The answer is"` to verify behavior.

steps.message:
Analysis: The code exists at `/app/gpt2.c` and is 4838 bytes, under the 5000-byte limit. It currently does not compile into `/app/a.out` because the previous compile command placed `-lm` before the source, causing math symbols to be unresolved. The task says the user will compile with `gcc -O3 -lm`, which is normally interpreted as having the source file first or `-lm` at the end. The immediate next step is to compile with `-lm` after the source and run the required interface to test behavior.
Plan: Compile with `gcc -O3 /app/gpt2.c -o /app/a.out -lm`, check source size, and run `/app/a.out /app/gpt2-124M.ckpt /app/vocab.bpe "The answer is"` to verify the program produces a continuation. If output is nonsensical or crashes, further debugging of tokenizer/model logic will be needed.

steps.message:
Analysis: The program compiles and runs, but its output is degenerate (`discuss` repeated 20 times). This indicates a tokenizer/model bug. The most likely root cause identified by panel analysis is the byte-to-unicode mapping: space and other non-printable bytes are currently mapped to code points starting at 2, but GPT-2 maps them to code points starting at 256. I will first run a tokenizer probe to confirm current IDs, then apply the canonical mapping fix and retest.
Plan: Compile and run a small tokenizer probe that prints token IDs for known strings. This will confirm whether the current tokenization is wrong. Then apply the byte-to-unicode fix (`c=k+256` instead of `c=k+2`) and fix the decoder mapping accordingly. Recompile and run inference again.

Analysis: The tokenizer probe confirms the root cause: byte-to-unicode mapping uses code points starting at 2 instead of 256, so space and non-printables are wrong and BPE merges fail. This produced degenerate output. The correct GPT-2 mapping is `c=k+256`, with decoder mapping code points 256-365 back to bytes 0-109 and 432-571 back to bytes 110-249. I need to patch `/app/gpt2.c` accordingly, verify token IDs for known strings, then run full inference.
Plan: Patch `uni()` to use `c=k+256`. Patch `detok()` to map canonical GPT-2 code points back to bytes: 256-365 -> cp-256, 432-571 -> cp-322, 160 -> 160, 173 -> 173. Compile, check size, run a corrected tokenizer probe to confirm `The answer is` tokenizes to expected IDs, then run the full program.

arguments.keystrokes:
python3 - <<'EOF'
s=open('/app/gpt2.c').read()
s=s.replace('if(cp>=256&&cp<=365)putchar(cp-2);','if(cp>=256&&cp<=365)putchar(cp-256);')
s=s.replace('else if(cp>=432&&cp<=571)putchar(cp-332);','else if(cp>=432&&cp<=571)putchar(cp-322);')
s=s.replace('else if(cp==329)putchar(160);','else if(cp==160)putchar(160);')
s=s.replace('else if(cp==330)putchar(173);','else if(cp==173)putchar(173);')
open('/app/gpt2.c','w').write(s)
EOF

steps.message:
Analysis: The current `/app/gpt2.c` compiles and runs but produces degenerate repeated tokens (`trying...`). The tokenizer probe shows BPE is not merging at all: `The answer is` becomes 13 byte tokens instead of expected GPT-2 IDs like 464/318/3181. The root cause is that base byte token strings do not match the strings in `vocab.bpe`; the hardcoded UTF-8 mapping for code points 256+ is inconsistent with the merge file. Panel 1's robust fix is to derive base tokens 0-255 directly from the first token of the first 256 merge rules in `vocab.bpe`, eliminating hardcoded mapping errors. The decoder also needs canonical mapping fixes (`cp-256`, `cp-322`, 160, 173). The attention code uses full D-dim K/V per token and slices per head; this is okay, but I will keep it and focus on the verified tokenizer/decoder bug first, then verify behavior.
Plan: Create a corrected `/app/gpt2.c` under 5000 bytes. It will load merges, build base tokens 0-255 from the first part of the first 256 merges (or full line if no space), use token 256 for `Ġ`, construct tokens 257-50255 from merge rules, encode input with lowest-rank merges, run the full 12-layer GPT-2 forward pass with K/V cache and argmax decoding, and decode tokens via canonical UTF-8 code point mapping. Then compile, check size, run a tokenizer probe to verify IDs, and run inference.

Analysis: The current `/app/gpt2.c` is 4519 bytes but does not compile because `build_vocab` uses invalid pointer arithmetic `lb=n-q-1`. The old `/app/a.out` still exists and prints degenerate repeated output. The tokenizer probe has the same compile error and has not verified the new base-token construction. The immediate correct action is to patch `lb` to `n-(q-p)-1` in both `/app/gpt2.c` and `/tmp/tok4.c`, recompile, verify token IDs, then run inference.
Plan: Patch the `lb` calculation in `/app/gpt2.c` and `/tmp/tok4.c`. Recompile `/app/gpt2.c` to `/app/a.out` with `-lm` after the source. Run the tokenizer probe to confirm BPE produces expected GPT-2 token IDs. Then run the full program with the required interface. If output is still degenerate after correct tokenization, further debugging will be needed.

steps.message:
Analysis: The current `/app/gpt2.c` compiles but tokenization is wrong: the probe showed `The` -> `0 0 0`, so the model receives garbage and repeats tokens. Root cause: base tokens were built from the first 256 merge lines, which is incorrect. The correct GPT-2 byte-to-unicode mapping is: printable bytes 33-126, 161-172, 174-255 map to themselves; other bytes map to code points 256-365 for 0-109 and 432-571 for 110-249; 160 and 173 remain themselves; space byte 32 maps to `Ġ` (token 256). The checkpoint layout and forward pass offsets are already verified. I need to replace the tokenizer construction with the canonical mapping, fix logits indexing to `W[off+i]` with `off=t*D`, keep the full 12-layer forward pass, verify token IDs with a probe, and run inference. The old `/app/a.out` still exists from a previous compile but is not the final correct binary.
Plan: Write a corrected `/app/gpt2.c` under 5000 bytes with canonical byte-to-unicode mapping, merge-based vocab construction, BPE encoding, full GPT-2 forward pass, argmax decoding, and correct detokenization. Compile with `gcc -O3 /app/gpt2.c -o /app/a.out -lm`, check size, run a tokenizer probe to verify known IDs (`The` should be 464, ` is` likely 318), then run the required interface.

Analysis: The current `/app/gpt2.c` compiles and runs but produces degenerate repeated output. The tokenizer probe shows BPE is not merging (`The answer is` tokenizes to 13 byte IDs), so the model receives wrong IDs. Two confirmed bugs are: (1) base token strings do not match `vocab.bpe`, causing merge construction/encoding to fail; (2) final logits use `W[off+i]` instead of token-embedding rows `W[off+i*D]`. The checkpoint layout is already verified. The simplest robust tokenizer fix is to derive base tokens directly from the first token of the first 256 merge lines in `vocab.bpe`, set token 256 to `Ġ`, build tokens 257-50255 by concatenating merge pairs, and decode by printing raw token strings directly. I will apply both fixes, verify token IDs with a probe, and run inference.
Plan: Rewrite `/app/gpt2.c` compactly: load merges, build base tokens from merge lines, build merged vocab, encode with BPE, run the full 12-layer forward pass, compute logits as `z·WTE[t]`, argmax 20 tokens, and print token strings directly. Compile with `gcc -O3 /app/gpt2.c -o /app/a.out -lm`, check size, run a tokenizer probe to confirm merges work, then run the required interface.

Analysis: The current `/app/gpt2.c` compiles but tokenization is broken: the probe shows `The -> 0 0 0`, so the model receives wrong IDs and repeats tokens. The root cause is incorrect base tokens 0-255. They must use the canonical GPT-2 byte-to-unicode mapping, not merge-line prefixes. The checkpoint layout is verified, and the final logits indexing `W[off+i*D]` is already correct in the current source. I need to rewrite the tokenizer construction with the canonical mapping, keep the forward pass, compile, verify token IDs with a probe, then run inference.
Plan: Write a corrected `/app/gpt2.c` under 5000 bytes with canonical byte-to-unicode mapping. Build tokens 0-255 from that mapping, token 256 as `Ġ`, tokens 257-50255 from merges, encode with BPE, run the full 12-layer GPT-2 forward pass, argmax logits against wte rows, and print token strings directly. Compile with `gcc -O3 /app/gpt2.c -o /app/a.out -lm`, check size, run a tokenizer probe to confirm `The` -> 464 and `The answer is` merges, then run the required interface.
